explanation: If prop_0 occurs (becomes true), then eventually prop_1 must happen afterwards. This is a basic causality/response pattern in LTL.

This requirement means that whenever prop_0 occurs, at some point afterwards prop_1 must occur. This is expressed by the LTL formula: globally, if prop_0 becomes true, then eventually prop_1 will become true.
Typically, to say prop_0 confirms prop_1 means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at that time. This can be expressed as 'globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must hold' in LTL.
The requirement states that the self-test procedure must perform prop_0. In LTL, this usually means that prop_0 is required to eventually occur.
The requirement is that the self-test procedure (represented by the atomic proposition prop_0) shall occur. In LTL, the notion that something shall happen is expressed as 'eventually'.
This requirement is typically understood as: whenever prop_1 happens, prop_0 shall eventually occur (as confirmation). In LTL, this is captured as: 'Globally, if prop_1 then eventually prop_0.'

The statement 'The prop_0 shall prop_1' is typically interpreted to mean that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold (either at the same time or possibly immediately after). The most straightforward interpretation in LTL, given only general operators and no comparison operators, is that always, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must be true in that state. This is expressed globally: G (prop_0 -> prop_1).

This requirement suggests that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 should have occurred before or occur at the same time (i.e., prop_1 is a precondition or simultaneous condition for prop_0), or possibly that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must eventually be true. A common LTL translation for such confirmation and response patterns is: globally, if prop_0 happens, then eventually prop_1 must happen.
The property states that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This is a safety property, typically expressed as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1'.

If 'confirm' means that whenever prop_1 occurs, it is eventually followed by prop_0, then in LTL: whenever prop_1 is true, eventually (in the future) prop_0 will be true. This is expressed as G(prop_1 -> F prop_0).
